examples.title: Basic Mount and Unmount Commands in Linux
examples.description: Common mount/umount commands used in Unix systems for attaching and detaching file systems
examples.code:
# Mount a device to a directory
mount /dev/sdb1 /mnt/usb

# Mount with specific options (read-only, noexec)
mount -o ro,noexec /dev/sdb1 /mnt/usb

# Mount a specific file system type
mount -t ext4 /dev/sdb1 /mnt/data

# Show currently mounted file systems
mount | grep /dev

# Unmount a file system
umount /mnt/usb

# Force unmount if busy
umount -f /mnt/usb

# Lazy unmount
umount -l /mnt/usb

# Bind mount
mount --bind /source /target
examples.explanation: These commands demonstrate basic mounting operations. The mount command attaches a file system to a directory, with options controlling behavior. Umount detaches the file system after ensuring data integrity.

examples.title: Configuring Automatic Mounting in /etc/fstab
examples.description: Static configuration file format for automatic mounting of file systems at boot
examples.code:
# /etc/fstab format:
# <device> <mount point> <type> <options> <dump> <fsck>

# Example entries:
/dev/sda1   /               ext4    defaults        0       1
/dev/sda2   /home           ext4    defaults        0       2
/dev/sdb1   /mnt/data       ntfs-3g defaults        0       2
server:/share /mnt/nfs      nfs     defaults        0       0

# Mount options explained:
# defaults = rw,suid,dev,exec,auto,nouser,async
# dump: 0 = no backup, 1 = backup
# fsck: 0 = no check, 1 = root, 2 = other partitions
examples.explanation: The /etc/fstab file defines which file systems to mount automatically at boot time. Each line specifies the device, mount point, file system type, mount options, and dump/fsck order for checking.

examples.title: Simulating Mount Operation in Python
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating the logical steps of a mount operation in an OS
examples.code:
class FileSystem:
    def __init__(self, device, mount_point, fs_type):
        self.device = device
        self.mount_point = mount_point
        self.fs_type = fs_type
        self.mounted = False
        
def mount(self):
        # Step 1: Validate device exists
        if not self.device_exists():
            raise Error("Device not found")
        
        # Step 2: Read and validate superblock
        superblock = self.read_superblock()
        if not self.validate_superblock(superblock):
            raise Error("Invalid file system")
        
        # Step 3: Allocate mount structure
        mount_struct = self.create_mount_struct(superblock)
        
        # Step 4: Add to mount table
        mount_table.add_entry(self.mount_point, mount_struct)
        
        # Step 5: Mark as mounted
        self.mounted = True
        return True
    
    def unmount(self):
        # Check if busy
        if self.is_busy():
            raise Error("Device busy")
        
        # Flush pending writes
        self.sync()
        
        # Remove from mount table
        mount_table.remove_entry(self.mount_point)
        
        # Release resources
        self.mounted = False
        return True
examples.explanation: This pseudocode shows the logical steps in mounting: device validation, superblock reading and validation, structure allocation, mount table update, and finally marking as mounted. Unmounting reverses this process with additional data integrity checks.

examples.title: System Call Implementation for Mounting
examples.description: C pseudocode showing how mount system call interacts with kernel data structures
examples.explanation: The mount() system call is the kernel interface for attaching file systems. It takes source device, target mount point, filesystem type, flags, and optional data. Different flags control mount behavior like read-only or bind operations.
